description: The core action-resolution engine of a game: the randomizer or judgment procedure that decides whether attempts succeed, how outcomes are graded and modified, and the currencies and target numbers that shape those decisions.

core_mechanic.description: The fundamental procedure — dice, cards, tokens, or judgment — used to resolve uncertain actions.

focuses.d20_vs_dc: Roll a twenty-sided die, add modifiers, and compare against a difficulty class or armor class set by the task or target (D&D 5e, Pathfinder).
focuses.dice_pool_successes: Roll a pool of dice sized by traits and count every die at or above a threshold as a success, with more successes meaning a better result (Shadowrun, Vampire: the Masquerade).
focuses.pbta_2d6_tiers: Roll 2d6 plus a stat and read three fixed bands: 6 or less misses with consequences, 7-9 succeeds with complications, 10+ succeeds cleanly (Apocalypse World and Powered by the Apocalypse games).
focuses.2d6_plus_skill: Roll 2d6, add skill and characteristic modifiers, and meet a standard target number, classically 8+ (Traveller).
focuses.roll_under_percentile: Roll d100 and succeed by rolling at or under a percentile skill rating (Call of Cthulhu, Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay, RuneQuest).
focuses.roll_under_attribute: Roll a die or dice and succeed by rolling under a raw attribute score (The Black Hack, GURPS-style 3d6 under skill).
focuses.blackjack_roll_under_high: Roll under your rating but as high as possible, so the best results land just below the cap and beat lower successful rolls (Pendragon, Unknown Armies).
focuses.opposed_rolls: Both sides of a contest roll and compare results directly, with the higher (or better-margin) roll winning the exchange.
focuses.card_draw: Actions resolve by drawing or playing cards from a standard or custom deck instead of rolling dice (Castle Falkenstein, Savage Worlds initiative).
focuses.tarot_draw: A tarot or bespoke oracle deck is drawn and interpreted to resolve actions or steer fate, with card meaning shaping the outcome (Everway's Fortune Deck, Invisible Sun's sooth deck).
focuses.jenga_tower: Players pull blocks from a physical tower to succeed at actions; toppling the tower triggers the character's doom (Dread).
focuses.diceless_bidding: Conflicts resolve by secretly or openly bidding from finite resource pools, with the higher committed bid prevailing (Amber Diceless Roleplaying).
focuses.diceless_gm_fiat: Outcomes are adjudicated by the GM comparing stated approaches, character capabilities, and fictional positioning with no randomizer at all (Amber outside auctions, freeform play).
focuses.rock_paper_scissors: Contests resolve with rock-paper-scissors throws, sometimes modified by traits or retests (Mind's Eye Theatre LARPs).
focuses.single_d6_tables: A single d6 is rolled and cross-referenced on a fixed outcome table or matrix, common in minimalist designs and classic board games.
focuses.step_dice: Traits are rated as die sizes (d4 up to d12), and improving a trait steps the die up; you roll your trait die against a target (Savage Worlds, Earthdawn).
focuses.roll_and_keep: Roll a number of dice, then keep only a subset (usually your best) and sum those for the result (Legend of the Five Rings).
focuses.wager_dice: Players stake dice or raise the difficulty before rolling to buy bigger effects at the risk of harder failure (7th Sea raises).
focuses.d100_criticals: A percentile engine where special results — doubles, 01, 00, or extreme margins — trigger critical successes, fumbles, or chart-based effects (Warhammer Fantasy Roleplay 4e doubles, Rolemaster crit tables).
focuses.dice_ladder: Numeric results translate onto a named ladder of quality tiers, so play talks in adjectives like Fair, Good, and Great rather than raw numbers (Fate's adjective ladder).
focuses.fate_fudge_dice: Roll four six-sided dice marked plus, minus, and blank, sum the symbols from -4 to +4, and add a skill to place the result on a ladder (Fate, Fudge).
focuses.symbol_dice: Custom dice carry narrative symbols — successes, advantages, threats, triumphs — that are cancelled and read in combination rather than summed (Genesys, FFG Star Wars).
focuses.chit_pull: Tokens or chits drawn blind from a bag or cup determine activation, events, or outcomes (classic hex-and-counter wargames, bag-builders like Orléans).
focuses.dice_placement: Dice are rolled and then allocated as workers or actions, with the face value gating what each die can do (Kingsburg, Alien Frontiers).
focuses.dice_drafting: Players take turns picking dice from a shared rolled pool, so resolution power flows through the draft rather than personal rolls (Sagrada, Grand Austria Hotel).
focuses.exploding_dice: Dice that roll their maximum value are rerolled and added, letting results open-end upward without limit (Savage Worlds aces, Shadowrun's rule of six).
focuses.push_your_luck: Keep rolling or drawing to accumulate a better result, risking a bust condition that forfeits some or all of the gains (Can't Stop, The Quacks of Quedlinburg).
focuses.highest_die_pool: Roll a pool of d6s and read only the single highest die against fixed bands, with multiple sixes upgrading to a critical (Blades in the Dark and Forged in the Dark games).
focuses.dice_matching_sets: Roll a pool and read matched sets, where the set's width and height determine speed and quality of the outcome (the One Roll Engine: Godlike, Wild Talents).
focuses.coin_toss_pool: Flip a pool of coins and count heads as successes against a difficulty (Prince Valiant).

degrees.description: How outcomes are graded beyond binary pass/fail, from partial successes to catastrophic fumbles.

focuses.success_with_cost: A roll can succeed while attaching a concrete cost, condition, or complication the player must accept (Mouse Guard twists, 'yes, but' resolution).
focuses.degrees_of_success: Results are read in named grades — such as critical success, success, failure, critical failure — each with distinct mechanical effects (Pathfinder 2e's four degrees, WFRP success levels).
focuses.margin_of_success: The numeric difference between roll and target scales the outcome continuously, with bigger margins meaning stronger effects (GURPS margin of success).
focuses.partial_success: A middle result band succeeds at the goal but with reduced effect, a hard bargain, or a complication attached (the 7-9 band in PbtA games).
focuses.critical_success: An extreme roll (natural 20, doubles, matching maximums) produces an exceptional result beyond a normal success.
focuses.critical_failure: An extreme low roll (natural 1, 00) produces a failure notably worse than normal, often with immediate mechanical fallout.
focuses.fumble_tables: Botched rolls send you to a random table that dictates exactly how the failure goes wrong (DCC fumble tables, Rolemaster fumble charts).
focuses.fail_forward: Failure never stalls play; a missed roll always moves the story by introducing a new problem, cost, or revelation instead of a dead end (Ironsworn's Pay the Price, GUMSHOE design philosophy).
focuses.raises: Beating the target by fixed increments grants stacked bonus effects, one per increment (Savage Worlds raises every 4 over the TN).
focuses.critical_confirmation: A potential critical must be confirmed by a second roll before its enhanced effect applies (Pathfinder 1e crit confirmation).
focuses.dual_axis_outcomes: Success/failure and positive/negative side effects are read on independent axes of the same roll, so you can fail with advantage or succeed with threat (Genesys success versus advantage).

modifiers.description: How circumstances, fiction, and assistance mathematically shift rolls before or after they are made.

focuses.advantage_disadvantage: Favorable or unfavorable circumstances have you roll twice and keep the better or worse die instead of applying numeric modifiers (D&D 5e advantage).
focuses.situational_modifiers: The GM applies itemized plus/minus adjustments for cover, lighting, tools, time pressure, and similar circumstances (GURPS, d20 circumstance bonuses).
focuses.aspect_invocation: Players spend a resource to invoke a relevant descriptive aspect for a bonus or reroll, and the GM compels aspects to complicate life (Fate aspects).
focuses.tag_based: Short descriptive tags on characters, gear, and scenes grant bonus dice or modifiers whenever a tag plausibly applies (City of Mist tags, 13th Age backgrounds).
focuses.positioning_effect: Before the roll, the table sets position (how risky) and effect (how much can be accomplished), which frames both stakes and payout (Blades in the Dark).
focuses.help_dice: Assisting characters add dice or bonuses to the acting character's roll, usually at some cost or shared risk to the helper (Blades in the Dark assists, 2d20 teamwork).
focuses.boost_setback_dice: Circumstances add whole positive or negative dice to the pool rather than flat numbers, keeping modifiers physical and composable (Genesys boost and setback dice).
focuses.condition_penalties: Ongoing states like wounds, fatigue, or hunger impose standing penalties on rolls until the condition is cleared (World of Darkness health levels, Forbidden Lands conditions).
focuses.scale_modifiers: Large mismatches in size, mass, or power band shift dice, damage, or difficulty categorically rather than incrementally (vehicle silhouettes in FFG Star Wars, scale in Godlike).
focuses.effort_spend: Players spend points from their own stat pools to lower a task's difficulty before rolling, converting reserves into reliability (Cypher System Effort).
focuses.escalation_die: A shared die ticks upward each round of a conflict, adding a growing bonus that pushes fights toward conclusion (13th Age escalation die).

metacurrency.description: Spendable meta-level currencies that let players and GMs bend rolls, buy rerolls, and trade narrative control.

focuses.bennies: Players earn tokens for good play and spend them for rerolls, soaking damage, or recovering, refreshed each session (Savage Worlds bennies).
focuses.fate_points: A refreshing pool spent to invoke aspects, declare story details, or resist compels, forming the game's core economy of narrative control (Fate points).
focuses.inspiration: A binary boon awarded for roleplaying traits, spent for advantage on one roll (D&D 5e Inspiration).
focuses.hero_points: A small pool of points spent for rerolls, extra actions, or cheating death, awarded for heroism or milestones (Pathfinder 2e hero points, Mutants & Masterminds).
focuses.momentum: Excess successes bank into a shared team pool that any player can spend to boost later actions (2d20 system: Star Trek Adventures, Conan).
focuses.threat_pool: The GM accumulates a visible pool — fed by player shortcuts or complications — and spends it to empower opposition or trigger setbacks (2d20 Threat, Marvel Heroic's doom pool).
focuses.xp_on_miss: Failed rolls directly award advancement, making misses a long-term investment (Dungeon World marking XP on a 6-).
focuses.devils_bargain: Before rolling, a player may accept a guaranteed complication in exchange for a bonus die (Blades in the Dark's Devil's Bargain).
focuses.plot_points: A currency spent to activate assets, create story facts, or step up dice, flowing between players and GM as complications are embraced (Cortex Prime plot points).
focuses.willpower_spend: Characters spend from a willpower trait for automatic successes, rerolls, or to resist compulsion, recovering it through rest or roleplay (Vampire: the Masquerade).
focuses.shared_pool_flow: A fixed set of tokens flows between a player-side and GM-side pool as it is spent, so every player boost eventually arms the opposition (Genesys story points).
focuses.gm_intrusions: The GM injects a complication into a scene and compensates the affected player with metacurrency or XP, which the player may refuse at a price (Cypher System GM intrusions).

target_numbers.description: How the number-to-beat is set, contested, scaled, and accumulated across rolls.

focuses.static_dc: Difficulties are fixed numbers drawn from a standard table (easy 10, hard 20), known or estimated before the roll (D&D difficulty classes).
focuses.variable_tn: The target number itself shifts with task difficulty or conditions rather than modifying the roll, so harder tasks raise the bar directly (Earthdawn step tables, L5R TNs).
focuses.contested_tn: The target is generated by the opposition — their roll, or a passive value derived from their stats — so difficulty tracks the opponent (opposed checks, D&D passive Perception).
focuses.clock_progress: Long or layered obstacles are drawn as segmented clocks that fill with successes and empty or advance with setbacks, resolving when full (Blades in the Dark clocks).
focuses.success_thresholds: Pool systems set difficulty as the number of successes required, not a per-die target, so hard tasks demand multiple hits (Shadowrun thresholds).
focuses.extended_tests: A task requires accumulating successes or margin across a series of rolls within limited attempts or time, letting effort build up or run out (Shadowrun extended tests).
focuses.player_facing_rolls: Only players roll; NPCs and hazards are expressed purely as difficulties or reactive effects, keeping all randomness on the player side (Cypher System, Ironsworn).
focuses.group_checks: The whole party rolls and the group succeeds if a set fraction of members pass, averaging out individual weakness (D&D 5e group checks).
focuses.tiered_difficulty_scale: Difficulty is set on a small abstract scale that converts to a target number by formula, so the table reasons in tiers rather than raw numbers (Cypher System's difficulty 1-10 times three).

luck.description: Explicit luck ratings and fortune resources that cushion, redirect, or gamble with fate.

focuses.luck_stat: Characters have a rated luck score that is itself rolled or drawn against when chance, not skill, decides an outcome (Call of Cthulhu Luck, DCC Luck).
focuses.reroll_resources: A small renewable stock of points is spent to reroll failed dice, refreshing on a fixed schedule such as daily or per session (WFRP Fortune points).
focuses.luck_rolls: The GM calls for a roll against luck to settle circumstantial questions — who the monster attacks, whether a taxi is passing — outside any skill's purview (Call of Cthulhu luck rolls).
focuses.luck_burn: Luck can be permanently spent down to boost rolls in an emergency, trading long-term fortune for immediate survival (DCC luck burn, Call of Cthulhu 7e spending Luck).
focuses.death_defiance_points: A tiny stock of destiny points can be burned only to escape certain death or maiming, permanently reducing the character's reserve of fate (WFRP Fate points, Dark Heresy).
